next_head_excerpt:
# Chapter 18

By first light the houses on Kadam Char stood open-backed. Doors leaned in tied bundles against lane walls, bedframes came out through holes where woven partitions had been cut loose, and the air held the hot metal smell of tin sheets recently pried from nails. Nothing looked abandoned yet. It looked worked on, stripped for parts, a place people were still trying to win from the river one cooking pot and one hinge at a time.

Halima's own house had lost its modesty overnight. Once the bedding came off the walls and the rice bin was emptied and the low shelf cleared of oil bottles, the room showed every stain the years had put there: lamp smoke above the niche, old birth blood scrubbed pale into one corner of the floor, the dark thumb-worn patch beside the doorway where she had steadied herself so often that the mud plaster had turned smooth as fired clay. She stood a moment with her broad palm over that place. The wall still held the night's cool. By noon it would be somebody else's rubble or the river's.

Nura was already at work, fast and mean with it because that was how she handled anything that could still hurt her. She had rolled the mosquito net tight enough to choke it, tied the bedding with sari cloth, wrapped her school certificates and the nursing forms inside two layers of plastic, then pushed the packet high into the tin trunk beneath Halima's old shawls. The small burn mark on her wrist flashed whenever she reached across the room.

"Don't put the scissors at the bottom," she said without looking up. "If they search the bags again, they'll make a...
open_hooks_to_carry: The chapter provides genuine quiet and communal baseline before the ending.
Material acts of dismantling homes parallel the book's emotional disassembly of lineage.
prev_tail_excerpt:
...le the magistrate sat there with his files and his demand for action, what the hearing had actually bought.

Most of Kadam Char would move now with money attached to its name. That was not small. Tin that would have been abandoned could be loaded. Debt that would have snapped a household in two might be postponed, bargained down, survived. Children would arrive at Nabinpara with at least one official line still holding under them. The list had done real work.

It had not kept a son from calling his mother by her true name until her body had already started shutting down. It had not kept a husband from stepping half a hand away from his wife while the village weighed her life out loud. It had not given Nura a clean father line, or Halima a house-site number, or anyone on the western edge a mercy that did not come stamped with exposure.

Outside, the island sounded busy, almost purposeful. Inside the yard, purpose had narrowed to wrapping what could still be wrapped.

Halima reached up to the loft shelf and laid her palm on the oilcloth over the ledger. The cloth was warm from trapped heat. Below her hand lived thirty years of names, substitutions, salvations, injuries. Around her, the char was already turning those names into bundles, departures, distances between spouses, debts for the dead, silence inside one excluded house.

She had managed to save the island more thoroughly than she had managed to save the people who made it. By noon that truth was visible everywhere a roof came down and everywhere two people spoke to each other differently than they had the week before.
state_deltas_required: Leave Kadam Char with a household intact enough to move, even if legal confirmation and emotional absolution remain incomplete.
Halima and Nura reach a partial reconciliation and decide to travel to the relocation site together even without a fully settled official status.
